**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling outside like the voices of the damned. The sea is restless, and the storm is upon us. I have not seen you in five years, and I fear that this may be the last night I shall ever see the light of day. Yet, as I sit here, the lantern still burns, and I must find the courage to speak to you, to tell you the truth, and perhaps, to ask for forgiveness.

Five years ago, I was a man of purpose, a guardian of the sea, a keeper of the light. But in that time, I have become a shadow of the man I once was. Our last conversation was bitter, filled with accusations and misunderstandings. You called me a monster, and I called you a child who had no idea what I was going through. I was wrong, and I know it now.

Tonight, as the storm threatens to tear down the lighthouse, I feel the weight of my past decisions pressing down on me. I have spent my life protecting others, yet I have failed to protect the one person who mattered most to me. I have allowed my duty to the sea to overshadow my duty to my family. I have let fear and pride keep me from reaching out to you.

I remember the day we last spoke. It was a stormy night, much

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the sea on this storm-laden night. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes me question whether I have the strength to see another dawn. I am not sure if I will survive this night, and if I do, I wonder if I will ever be able to speak to you again.

Five years have passed since your last letter, and I have not replied. I know you have been waiting, and I know you have been hurt. I do not blame you. I have no excuse, only the weight of my silence and the burden of my pride. You were right to be angry, and I was wrong to let pride stand between us.

I have always believed that a man must stand tall, that a father must be strong. But strength, I have come to see, is not in silence, but in the courage to speak when it matters most. I have been a fool, Eleanor, and I have let my stubbornness blind me to the truth that you are my daughter, and I am your father.

This storm is not just a test of my strength—it is a test of my soul. I have spent many nights staring into the darkness, wondering if I have done enough, if I have made a difference in your life. I have watched the world change, and I have seen you grow into a woman

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

---

**Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long, storm-laden years since we last spoke, and now, as the sky darkens with the promise of a tempest that will test the very soul of this lighthouse, I find myself compelled to write. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that mirrors the tempest within me.

I have kept the light burning all night, as I always have, through the years of silence, through the years of regret, through the years of longing. But tonight feels different. Tonight, the storm is not just a test of my duty—it is a reckoning.

You were never the daughter I wanted to raise. You were the daughter I failed to understand. I was a man of routine, of duty, of the sea and stone, and you were a child of fire, of passion, of dreams that burned too bright. I feared your ambition, your defiance, your desire to leave the lighthouse and the life I had built. I did not know how to reach you, how to hold you close without smothering your spirit.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself wondering—what if I had been wrong? What if I had been too afraid to let you go? What if I had been too stubborn to listen?

I have spent these years watching the waves

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear the winds of time have not been kind to me. Five years since we last spoke, and yet the weight of silence still sits heavy upon my heart. I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the hope that you might one day understand the sorrow that has kept me from your side.

The storm that approaches tonight is no mere tempest of wind and rain—it is a beast of fury, a thing that has not been seen in these parts for many a year. The sea roars like a beast in pain, and the skies are dark as the abyss. I have stood upon these cliffs for many years, and I have weathered countless storms, but this one... this one feels different. The waves grow taller, the winds more fierce, and I fear that even the old lighthouse may not stand against the wrath of the night.

I have taken all necessary precautions, as I always do. The lamp is primed, the oil is fresh, and the beacon is set to burn with its full light. Should the storm claim me, I pray that the light will guide those who sail these waters, just as it has guided me through the darkest of nights. I have always believed that the light is a gift from God, and that it is our duty to keep it burning for those who follow the sea.

I know that

**April 3rd, 1887**  
**Lighthouse of Fairhaven Point**  
**Cape Whitmore, Scotland**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this by the flickering light of the beacon, the wind howling like a wounded beast in the night. The sea is restless, the waves crashing against the cliffs with a fury that makes me fear the very stones beneath my feet. I have been here for more than thirty years, and never before has the storm seemed so close, so dangerous. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn.

You were never meant to be a stranger, my child. You were never meant to walk away. I have stood here, night after night, watching the horizon, waiting for the light to guide the lost, when all the while, I have been waiting for you to come back.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of words left unsaid. I have tried to forget, but I cannot. You are still in my mind, in my heart, in the hush of the wind that whispers through the tower. I have heard it tonight, Eliza—those whispers. And I have felt the weight of this silence, heavier than any storm.

I have always been the keeper, the solitary man with a lantern and a compass, the man who never looked away from the dark. But now, as the sky darkens and the sea

**Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than my soul. Five long years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and though the silence between us has grown thick as the fog that curls around these rocks, I find myself compelled to speak—though my voice may not be the one you remember.

Tonight, the sea howls like a beast in mourning, and the wind bites with the cold of a thousand winters. The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, though I fear the storm may take more than the sea from me. I have spent the day preparing the lamp, trimming the wick, and ensuring that the beacon will shine through the darkness, a beacon not just for ships, but for those who may be lost in the night.

I have not written this letter with the hope that you will read it. I have written it with the hope that, should I not return, it may reach you and remind you of the man I once was—before the sea stole my words, before the silence grew too deep, and before the weight of years turned love into something unrecognizable.

But if I do live through this, I will find a way to return to you, even if it is only in the form of a letter. I will return, and I will not let the sea take my voice from you.

**Yours, in sorrow and longing,**

**Ephraim T. Harrow

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside the lighthouse, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old stone tower. The sea is restless, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, heavy with the promise of ruin. I do not know if I will see dawn, or if I will be swallowed by the storm like so many before me.

Five years have passed since you left, and in that time, I have not spoken a word to you. Not a single word. I have kept the lighthouse, kept the light burning, kept the sea at bay with my duty and my sorrow. But I have not kept you.

I know you do not want to hear from me. You have your own life, your own path, your own heart. And I have done nothing to deserve it. I have been a failure as a father, a man, a husband. I have let you go, and I have not tried to find you again.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and I feel the weight of the world pressing down upon me, I feel the need to say these things. To tell you, in the only way I know how, that I have never stopped loving you.

I have missed you more than words can say. I have missed your laughter, your voice, the way you looked at me with that quiet, unshakable strength. I have

My dearest Eliza,

It has been five long, weary years since your last visit, and I have not heard a word from you since. The sea, in its infinite wisdom, has brought me more sorrow than I care to count, and I find myself wondering if you have forgotten me, as the tide forgets the shore when the moon is far away.

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that would make even the bravest sailor quiver. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse before the night is done. But I must write, for if I do not, I shall be left with only the echoes of my own thoughts and the silence of the sea.

You were always a spirited child, Eliza, with a mind as sharp as the edge of a cutlass and a heart as full as the tide at high water. I have often wondered if you have grown into a woman of strength and purpose, or if you have been led astray by the wicked ways of the city. I do not know what manner of world you have been living in, but I pray to the Good Lord that you have not forgotten the ways of our kind—of honesty, of duty, and of the sea.

I have been keeping the light burning all these years, as I always have, though the lamp has long since gone out and the beacon is

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Heart, My Light, My Only Child,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heavy soul, for the storm that now gathers over the cliffs and the sea is not merely one of wind and wave, but of sorrow and silence. Five long years have passed since your last letter, and in that time, the world has turned, the tides have risen, and the stars have shifted. Yet I have not forgotten you, not for a single moment.

You were once my greatest pride, my daughter of the sea, my light in the darkness. I have watched the hours pass in the lonely vigil of the lighthouse, the lamp burning through night after night, the beacon guiding the lost and the weary. And yet, with all the light I have given the world, I have found myself in the deepest darkness of all—without your voice, your presence, your love.

I have not written to you in this time, for I feared that my words would be unworthy of your ears, or that my heart would be too heavy to bear the weight of your absence. But now, as the storm approaches with a fury that even the sea itself cannot resist, I feel the need to reach out, not to beg for your return, but to offer you my soul, my truth, and the truth of my heart.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I have seen countless storms. I have stood

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these walls. The sea is restless, and the storm is coming—a beast of fury that I fear will not spare us. I have kept the light burning all night, as I always have, though I know the lantern may not hold against the gales. If I do not return, know that I did not leave without a fight.

Five years have passed since you last called me *father*. Time has worn the words from my lips, but not from my heart. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the silence that followed. You were young, and I was too old to understand the weight of your choices. But I have come to understand that even the strongest of us must sometimes turn away from the light.

If you are reading this, then perhaps the storm has taken me. Perhaps the sea has claimed what I could not give you. I have no regrets, only the sorrow of a man who once believed he could be more than a keeper of the light.

I leave this letter with the lantern, in the hopes that it may find its way to you. If you ever return, know that the light still burns, and so does my love.

**Yours, always, though the world has not heard it in years,**  
**Thomas**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass of my window. The sea has grown restless, and the sky is a bruised canvas of black and green. I do not know if I will live to see the dawn, but if I do, I must tell you this: I have not stopped thinking of you, not once. Not for a single moment.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of silence that has grown heavier with each passing day. I have tried to fill the void with the work of the lighthouse, with the rhythm of the waves, with the company of the sea. But the sea does not speak to me now. It only watches. It only waits.

I have not spoken to you in five years, but I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the sound of your laughter, the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of the stars. I have not forgotten the way you used to run through the garden, barefoot and wild, as if the world was yours to run from and to.

I have not forgotten that you were my daughter. That you were the light I once thought would never fade.

But I fear I have become a shadow of the man I once was. I have grown old in this place, Eleanor. I have grown tired. The lighthouse has become my prison, and I have become its keeper, though

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in the midst of your own quiet, contemplative evenings, perhaps by the hearth with a cup of tea, or perhaps in the company of your own children, as I once was. I write to you now, not with the hope of rekindling the fire that once bound us, but with the solemnity of a man who has spent many years in solitude, and who now finds himself at the precipice of a storm that may take his life.

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and I fear that time has not been kind to either of us. I do not know whether you have heard the news of my father’s passing, or if you have already forgotten the name of your own mother. I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the bitter words we exchanged. I have not forgotten the way your eyes turned away from mine, as though I were a stranger, and not the man who once held you in his arms, who once taught you to read, and who once told you that you were the light of my life.

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**Opening:**
My dear daughter,  
It has been five long years since we last spoke. Time has worn away our connection, and the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. Tonight, as I sit in the lighthouse, the storm is gathering on the horizon. The wind howls like a beast, and the sea roars with fury. I am not sure if I will see dawn. But if I do, I must write this letter — not just to you, but to the man I once was, and the father I have become.

**Body:**
The lighthouse has always been my sanctuary, a place of solitude and purpose. But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself haunted by memories of you — of our last conversation, of the words left unsaid, of the pain that drove us apart. I have spent these five years in silence, but now, as the storm threatens to consume me, I feel the weight of my regrets. I have been a poor father, a man who let pride and distance cloud my judgment. I have not been there for you, and I know that I have lost you. But I want to make amends, even if it is too late.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old lighthouse, and I can scarcely keep the lamp alight against the tempest. But even the fiercest storm cannot drown the silence that has grown between us.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years since you left. I have watched the sea rage and the sky blacken, yet none have ever felt as dark as the chasm that now divides us. I have kept this lighthouse, as I have kept my silence. But tonight, the storm is different. I fear it may be my last.

I do not know if you will read these words, nor if you will even care. But I must say them, for the sake of my own peace. You were young, and I was too proud to see the truth in your eyes. I saw a child, not a woman. I saw a dreamer, not a daughter. And so I turned my back, not because I did not love you, but because I did not know how to love you in the way you needed.

You left, as you always had. Not with anger, but with a quiet, aching resolve. You believed I did not understand, and I fear I did not. I was bound to this stone, to this lamp, to the sea that has always been

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

---

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I sit here by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, and I find myself compelled to write. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But before I go, I must speak to you.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke. Time has worn down the edges of our lives, and the silence between us has grown heavier than the sea. I have tried to reach you, but the sea has kept our words from meeting. And now, as the tempest gathers, I feel the weight of that silence pressing upon me like the storm itself.

You were once my light, Eliza. You were the reason I built this lighthouse, the reason I stood watch through the darkest nights. You were my daughter, my pride, my hope. But the world changed, and so did we. You left, and I stayed, bound by duty, by the sea, by the light.

I have not spoken to you in five years. Not a word. Not a letter. Not a call. I have tried to forget, but the memory of your face, your laughter, your voice—those things remain. I have tried to be a father, but I have failed you. I have tried to be a man, but I have

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may find you in a temper, as I have been known to be when I am not in the best of spirits. I write to you now with a heavy heart, for the storm that brews upon the horizon is of a most fearsome nature, and I fear it may claim me before dawn.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you would even read these words. Yet, as the winds grow fiercer and the sea roars with a voice that echoes the sorrow in my soul, I feel compelled to reach out once more. I have spent these years in solitude, tending to the light and watching the waves, and though I have grown old, I have not forgotten the love I once held for you.

I have not written to you in so long because I feared the words would not be enough to mend what has been broken between us. I know I have been a poor father, and I regret the pain I have caused you. I have not been there for you when you needed me most, and I pray that you may one day forgive me for that.

If you should receive this letter and find me still alive, I beg you to come to the lighthouse. I have prepared a small meal, and I would have you stay with me until the storm passes. There is something I wish to say to you,

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Blackmoor Point]**

**Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since I last called you "my girl." I have kept the lighthouse, as I have always done—watchful, silent, steadfast. But the silence has grown long, and the watch has grown lonely. I have not spoken to you, and I have not spoken to myself.**

**I have not spoken to you, but I have thought of you. Every night, when the sea howls and the wind screams like a beast in pain, I hear your voice. I see your face. I have not been the father you deserved. I have been a man who feared his own heart, who let duty and duty alone speak for him. But tonight, I am not the lighthouse keeper who once stood guard over the sea — I am a man who has seen the sea claim many, and who knows that the light must be kept burning, even when the world is dark.

The winds have howled, the waves have crashed, and the night has grown longer and darker than ever before. **To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of silence. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I find myself at a loss for words. The sea has always been my companion, my solace, my burden. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the wind howls like a wounded beast, I feel the need to reach out—to you, my daughter, who once was my light in the darkest hours.

I have not seen you in years, and I do not know if you will even read this. If you do, I hope you will forgive me. I have been a poor father, and I have failed you in many ways. I know you must think me a cruel man, and I do not deny it. I have been distant, cold, and unworthy of your love. But I ask you to understand—this life, this duty, has consumed me. The lighthouse is not just a place; it is a part of me. I have given it my all, and in doing so, I have lost sight of the things that truly matter.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. Even now, as I sit here, I can almost hear your voice, reminding me of the warmth of home, the laughter of a family, the comfort of a mother’s embrace. I miss you more than I can say. I miss your laughter, your kindness,

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

---

**Dearest Eliza,**

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars with fury. I sit here by the light, the great beacon of my life, watching the storm gather on the horizon. It is not just the tempest that has come to visit me tonight—it is the storm within me, the storm of regret, of silence, of a heart that has long since given up on you.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face. Five years of silence, of missed birthdays, of unspoken words. You left me, Eliza. Not in a dramatic exit, not with a final goodbye, but in the quiet, slow erosion of connection. You stopped answering my letters. You stopped visiting. You stopped being part of my life. And I, in my stubborn pride, let it happen.

I have spent these years alone, tending to the lighthouse, the light, the sea. I have become a man of the rocks, of the wind, of the waves. A man who no longer knows how to reach out. But tonight—tonight, the storm is here. And I feel it in my bones that this may be the last night I have left.

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these walls, and the sea roars as though it seeks to devour the very earth. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for many a stormy night, and though the light is steady, my hands tremble with the cold and the weight of the years.

Five long years since we last spoke, and I have not written, nor have you answered. I have tried, my dear, I have tried to forget the day you left, the way your eyes turned cold as the sea on a winter's morn. **April 12th, 1893**  
**The Lighthouse, Cliffs of Wexham**  
**County of Devon, England**  

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast and the sea roaring as if it would swallow the very earth beneath us. The sky is a bruised violet, the waves clawing at the rocks with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The lighthouse stands as it always has — steadfast, unyielding, a sentinel of the storm. But I, who have stood here for so many years, now feel as if I am but a shadow of the man I once was.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since we last spoke. Five years since you left. I have not slept in days, not since the first signs of the storm came upon us. The sea has been restless, and so have I.

I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you will even believe it. But I must say it, for I have said it in my heart so many times, and never have the words come with the weight they carry now.

You were my light, Eliza. My only light. You were the daughter I never knew how to be a father to, and I was the man who never knew how to be a father at all. I was too proud, too silent, too afraid to reach out

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

> *To my dear, wayward daughter,*

I write this with the storm howling at my back, the sea rising in fury, and the light of the lighthouse flickering like the last ember of a dying fire. I have not spoken to you in five long years, and yet, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write. Not to justify, not to plead, but to speak the truth that has long lain buried in my heart—truth that has weathered the years like the stone of this very lighthouse.

You were once my light, my solace, my greatest joy. But time, and the weight of unspoken words, has turned you into a ghost in my memory. I have watched you drift away, and I have let you go. Yet now, as I stand on the precipice of fate, I find that I cannot bear to be forgotten entirely.

I have not been a good father. I was too stern, too distant, too lost in the duties of this lonely post. But I have not been cruel. I have only ever loved you in silence, in the flicker of a lamp, in the hush of the wind. And now, as the sea roars and the sky weeps, I must speak—though it may be my last words.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this letter with a heavy heart, my hands trembling from the cold and the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and though I have not spoken to you, I have not forgotten. The sea has always been my constant, but even the sea has its limits, and I fear this storm may be one of them.

I have done this for so many years, not for the recognition, nor for the praise, but because I have always believed that in the darkest hours, a light is the only thing that can guide a soul home.

I have not written to you, nor have I sent a word, for I feared what words might bring. But now, as the storm approaches and I stand alone in this tower, I feel the need to say something—anything—to you.

I have never been the kind of father who could sit and talk, who could ease the pain of a child’s heart with kind words or gentle promises. But I have always tried, in my own way, to be there for you. I have watched you grow from a little girl into a woman, and though I did not understand the path you chose, I have never ceased to love you.

My dearest Eliza,

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay you will find it lacking in the usual comforts of a proper correspondence. I have been meaning to write for some time, but the sea has been restless of late, and my thoughts have been preoccupied with the affairs of the lighthouse and the ever-present threat of the tempest.

You may think me cruel to have not written in these five years, but I assure you, it is not for want of care. I have been busy, as one must be when the wind howls like a banshee and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that would make a sailor weep. You must understand, my dear, that I am a man of routine and duty. The lighthouse is not a place for idle chatter or sentimental nonsense. It is a place for vigilance, for the salvation of ships that might otherwise be lost to the abyss.

I have heard from the postman, or rather, I have caught him in the act of delivering a letter—though I fear the ink is smudged and the paper is damp. He said you have taken up with a man of questionable character, and I do not like that. You should have stayed with your mother and me, where you would have been properly raised. A woman must know her place, and a daughter must be obedient to her father, no matter the cost.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, and I have not written to you, and yet I think of you often, as one might think of a lost star in the night sky.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of regret and the tremor of a hand that has long since lost its steadiness. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the cliffs whole. I have stood at the helm of this light for thirty years, but never have I felt so small, so alone.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never again. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself compelled to write, to say what words have long been buried beneath the weight of silence.

You were a child when you left, and I did not understand. I thought I was protecting you, shielding you from the world’s cruel edges. But I was wrong. I was afraid. Afraid of what you might become, of what I might become. And in my fear, I lost you.

I have watched the light burn through the darkness, steady as the heart of a man who still believes in something greater than himself. But tonight, the sea is restless. I have seen the signs—waves that rise like fists, the sky dark as a funeral shroud. I do not know if I will see dawn.

If I do not, know this: I have never stopped loving you. I have never stopped hoping you might one day return. And if you do, I will be here, waiting, as I always have been.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, shaking the very stones of this old tower. It has been five long years since you last came to visit, and five years since I last called you my daughter. The world has moved on, and I fear I have not. The sea, once a source of solace, now seems to whisper of your absence.

Tonight, a storm is coming—great and terrible, as if the sky itself is weeping. I’ve spent the evening preparing, lighting the lamp, ensuring the beacon burns with its steady light. It is my duty, as it has always been. But I wonder, as I do every night, if I’m still doing it for the right reasons. If the light is for the sailors, or if it is for someone else.

I have not spoken to you in years, Eliza. I have not written, nor have I answered your letters. I know you must have written. I know you must have waited. And I know I have not been worthy of your patience. I have been a fool, a coward, and a man who has let the world and his own heart grow cold. You deserved better than a father who has forgotten how to love.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a long time. I see your face in the mist, in the waves,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Daughter,**

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars with fury, as if the very heavens are in a rage. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see the dawn. Yet, I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I must try, with the last of my strength, to make amends.

You were always a fine girl, Eliza. I have no doubt of that. You were a bright light in my life, though I did not always treat you as such. I have been a fool, a grizzled old man, too proud and too stubborn to see the truth of your heart. You were not the daughter I wanted, nor the daughter I deserved. I allowed my pride to blind me, and for that, I am truly sorry.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and I have not heard a word from you. I have written, I have sent letters, and I have received none in return. I know you must have received them, for I have not been so careless as to lose them. Yet, you have not answered. I have no idea if you still live, or if you have found some new life in the world beyond this lonely shore. I have no idea if you have found love, or if you have found peace.
